Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a proper study routine. I'd just kind of drift through the weeks, maybe studying when I remembered or when something felt urgent. With 13 online courses going on, everything blurred together and I never really sat down to plan things out. I'd study in bursts, usually late at night when the anxiety kicked in about falling behind. It wasn't organized at all, more like survival mode most of the time.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything, then suddenly cram when deadlines were breathing down my neck. Sometimes I'd have a good week where I'd study a bit each day, but that never lasted. Life stuff kept getting in the way, and honestly, I just couldn't find the motivation to stick to a schedule even when I tried.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline notification popping up on my phone or that nagging anxiety that I was missing something important. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything new, scrolling through without really engaging. The gamified tasks actually got me logging in more because there was something almost satisfying about completing those little challenges, even when I felt disconnected from everything else.
Q4: The gamified stuff made it feel more routine for a while. Getting those achievement notifications gave me a small dopamine hit that kept me coming back. But outside of that, it never really became automatic for me. I'd have to consciously remind myself to check the LMS, and even then, I'd often just skim through things without really diving deep.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful because I learn better by seeing things visually. I could pause, rewind, and watch at my own pace, which helped when my concentration was shot. The peer interaction features were surprisingly helpful too – seeing other students' perspectives made me feel less alone in struggling with the material. Those gamified tasks also kept things interesting when everything else felt like a chore.
Q6: I mostly ignored the discussion posts unless I absolutely had to participate. Reading through long forum threads felt exhausting, and I never felt like I had anything valuable to add. Some of the supplementary readings I just skipped entirely because I was already overwhelmed with the core content. When you're low on resources and time, you have to pick what matters most.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it counted toward my grade. The gamified elements helped because completing tasks gave instant feedback and that sense of progress. If something felt too abstract or disconnected from what I needed to pass, I'd lose interest quickly. I needed to see the point, you know?
Q8: Optional stuff felt like a luxury I couldn't afford mentally. Required activities got done because they had to, but optional ones? I'd look at them and think 'maybe later' and that later never came. There's something about knowing something is mandatory that forces you through the anxiety barrier. Optional activities just didn't have enough pull when I was already struggling to keep up with the basics.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download PDFs and videos to study offline because my internet isn't always reliable and data costs money. I'd take screenshots of important slides and review them on my phone during commutes or when I couldn't sleep. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer, just me and my notes or downloaded files.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be going over my handwritten notes or reviewing screenshots for hours, but none of that would show up anywhere. Sometimes I'd watch a downloaded lecture multiple times, pausing to really understand something, but the LMS would only show I accessed it once. My actual engagement was way higher than what the system could track.
Q11: All the time. I'd open a video or document once to download it, then study from that file repeatedly over the next few days or weeks. It was more practical for me given my situation with limited resources and unreliable internet. The LMS would show one access, but I might have reviewed that material ten times offline.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where everything felt pointless. My anxiety would spike and I'd just shut down, couldn't bring myself to open anything course-related. Sometimes sadness would hit me out of nowhere and I'd spend days just existing, not really engaging with anything. Financial stress and feeling like I was drifting without direction made it hard to care about coursework.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from worrying about money or just feeling overwhelmed by having 13 courses, the LMS became this thing I dreaded opening. Confusion about what to prioritize made me freeze up sometimes. I'd log in, feel the weight of everything I hadn't done, and just close it again. Fatigue made everything feel ten times harder than it probably was.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered somewhere else entirely. Scrolling through materials without actually reading them, just going through the motions. My eyes would be on the screen but my brain was checked out, consumed by anxiety or just feeling empty. The activity was there but the learning wasn't.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially stuff that felt overwhelming or confusing. The anxiety would build up around certain tasks and avoiding them felt like the only way to cope in the moment. I knew it would make things worse later, but present-me couldn't handle facing it. It's like my brain chose short-term relief over long-term success every time.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd ignore stuff for weeks, then panic-work right before something was due. The LMS activity probably spiked massively around deadline periods and flatlined in between. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was the only thing that could cut through my low motivation and make me actually do something.
Q17: Feedback helped when it was encouraging – it gave me a small boost that maybe I wasn't completely failing. But sometimes negative feedback would send me spiraling, making me avoid the course for days. The gamified feedback was easier to handle because it felt less personal somehow. Getting those achievement badges made me feel like I was making progress even when everything else felt stuck.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and overwhelming. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me something to work toward, but with so many courses, keeping track of everything was impossible. The gamified elements added structure that actually motivated me more than traditional assignments. Without that, I probably would have drifted even more than I did.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier – they broke things into smaller, achievable chunks that didn't feel as daunting. But some aspects, like lengthy discussion requirements, made engagement harder because they demanded energy I didn't have. Visual content was easier for me to engage with than walls of text. The course could have used more interactive elements to keep people like me hooked.
Q20: I think those students might be like me – logging in, clicking around, maybe watching videos but not really absorbing anything. High anxiety can make you engage superficially without actually producing work. Or they might be struggling with something personal that prevents them from completing assignments even though they're trying to keep up. Activity doesn't equal productivity.
Q21: That actually describes me somewhat. My attendance was terrible and my login frequency wasn't amazing, but I still managed a Merit grade. I did a lot of work offline that the system couldn't see. Plus, when I did engage, I made it count – focusing on what would actually affect my grade. Sometimes quality matters more than quantity of logins.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns – when you're active, how often you return, what you click on. But they miss everything that happens offline, all the thinking and reviewing you do away from the screen. They can't measure understanding or emotional state. They show the shadow of engagement, not the substance. A stressed student cramming looks the same as a motivated student reviewing.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning, feeling connected to the material and the process. It's not just showing up or clicking through – it's when your brain is actually working on understanding something. For me, real engagement happened in rare moments when the anxiety quieted down and I could focus. Those gamified tasks created more of those moments than anything else.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much I struggled emotionally just to open it some days. It can't capture the hours I spent studying downloaded materials offline, or the mental energy I put into overcoming anxiety to complete assignments. It doesn't show that despite feeling sad and unmotivated, I still pushed through and got decent grades. The human cost of my engagement is completely invisible in the data.
